disclaimer: Operational employment/labor compliance research and cited public-records compilation — not legal advice. Confirm wage-hour, OSHA, EEOC, and state labor obligations with licensed counsel and the relevant agency before relying on any fact for operations or filings.

1_tldr.title: TL;DR / Executive Summary
1_tldr.coverage: Federal DOL FLSA wage-hour, OSHA workplace safety, EEOC hiring discrimination + state overlays for 10 high-volume states (CA, TX, NY, FL, PA, IL, OH, GA, NC, MI).
1_tldr.honesty_rule: Every claim STD-CITE-001 cited; gaps render NOT FOUND — [source checked: …].

2_scope_methodology_as_of.title: Scope, Methodology & As-Of Date
2_scope_methodology_as_of.methodology: Curated public-source harvest 2026-06-19. Primary federal = eCFR Title 29 + DOL/OSHA/EEOC .gov; state = statute and agency labor portals.

sample_facts: California minimum wage and overtime rules are set by Labor Code and IWC wage orders; employers must pay the higher of federal or state minimum and follow daily overtime after 8 hours and double-time rules for extended shifts.
Meal and rest break requirements apply to nonexempt employees; failure to provide compliant breaks triggers premium pay obligations under state law.

The Fair Employment and Housing Act (FEHA) prohibits harassment and discrimination in employment based on protected classes including race, religion, sex, disability, age, and others; DFEH/CRD enforces.
California employers must secure workers' compensation insurance or qualify as self-insured before hiring employees; failure to carry coverage is a criminal misdemeanor.
Employers must provide paid sick leave accrual or front-loading under the Healthy Workplaces, Healthy Families Act; poster and wage-statement requirements apply.
Employers with California wages must register with EDD for unemployment and disability insurance, file quarterly wage reports, and display mandatory workplace posters.
Texas does not set a state minimum wage above the federal floor; covered employers follow FLSA minimum wage and overtime unless a higher federal contract or local rule applies.
Texas Payday Law requires employers to pay wages at least twice monthly for non-exempt employees and on schedule for exempt employees; final pay timing rules apply on separation.

Chapter 21 of the Texas Labor Code prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, disability, religion, sex, national origin, and age; TWC Civil Rights Division enforces for smaller employers.
Private employers in Texas may elect workers' compensation coverage; non-subscribers lose common-law defenses and must notify employees and post notices of non-coverage.
Texas has no statewide paid sick leave mandate for private employers; local paid-sick ordinances have been preempted — confirm current preemption statute before relying on municipal rules.
Employers liable under the Texas Unemployment Compensation Act must register with TWC, report wages quarterly, and pay unemployment tax.
New York minimum wage varies by region and employer size under Labor Law Article 19; employers must post wage rates and comply with spread-of-hours and call-in pay rules where applicable.
Nonexempt employees receive overtime at 1.5× regular rate after 40 hours in a payroll week; certain residential and farm workers have distinct thresholds.

The New York State Human Rights Law prohibits discrimination in hiring and employment based on protected characteristics including age, race, creed, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, disability, and others.
New York employers must obtain workers' compensation insurance before hiring employees; failure to secure coverage exposes employers to penalties and personal liability.
Employers must provide paid sick leave accrual under Labor Law §196-b; amount depends on employer size; posting and recordkeeping required.
Paid Family Leave provides job-protected, paid time off for eligible employees; employers must display PFL workplace poster and collect employee contributions.
Employers must register with NY DOL for unemployment insurance, file quarterly wage reports, and maintain UI posters.
Florida minimum wage is adjusted annually by constitutional amendment; covered employers must pay the state minimum where it exceeds the federal floor and follow FLSA overtime rules.
The Florida Civil Rights Act prohibits employment discrimination because of race, color, religion, sex, pregnancy, national origin, age, handicap, or marital status.
Florida employers with four or more employees (construction: one or more) must secure workers' compensation coverage through an insurer or self-insurance.
Florida does not mandate private-sector paid sick leave statewide; employers must still comply with federal FMLA where applicable and post USERRA/OSHA/EEO notices.
Employers must register with Florida Reemployment Assistance (UI) tax program and report wages quarterly.
Pennsylvania follows the federal minimum wage for most employees; state overtime rules align with FLSA for nonexempt workers unless a higher standard applies under contract or local rule.

The Pennsylvania Human Relations Act prohibits discrimination in hiring and employment based on race, color, religious creed, ancestry, age, sex, national origin, disability, and other protected classes.
Pennsylvania employers must carry workers' compensation insurance for all employees, including part-time and seasonal workers, with limited exceptions.
Pennsylvania has no statewide paid sick leave mandate for private employers; Philadelphia and Pittsburgh have local ordinances — confirm locality before relying on state-wide absence of mandate.
Employers must register for Pennsylvania UC tax, file quarterly wage and tax reports, and display UC poster requirements.
Illinois Minimum Wage Law sets a state minimum wage above the federal floor; employers must pay the higher applicable rate and comply with overtime after 40 hours in a workweek.
Illinois requires meal periods for employees working 7.5+ hours and additional break rules for hotel room attendants and other listed occupations.

The Illinois Human Rights Act prohibits discrimination in employment based on protected classes including race, color, religion, sex, national origin, ancestry, age, disability, and others; IDHR enforces.
Illinois employers must provide workers' compensation insurance covering medical expenses and wage replacement for work-related injuries; failure to insure is a Class 4 felony.
The Paid Leave for All Workers Act requires most Illinois employers to provide paid leave for any reason; accrual and front-loading rules apply by employer size.
Employers must register with IDES for unemployment insurance, report wages quarterly, and pay UI taxes on covered employment.
Ohio minimum wage is indexed to inflation under a constitutional amendment; non-tipped employees receive the state rate where it exceeds the federal floor.
Ohio follows federal FLSA overtime standards for most private employers; state wage-hour division enforces minimum wage and prevailing-wage on public improvements.
Ohio Revised Code Chapter 4112 prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, military status, national origin, disability, age, and ancestry.
Ohio employers with one or more employees must obtain workers' compensation coverage through the state insurance fund or self-insurance/BWC alternative.
Ohio has no statewide paid sick leave mandate for private employers; municipal ordinances may apply — confirm locality (REQUIRES_CONFIRMATION for city rules).
Employers must register with Ohio Department of Job and Family Services for unemployment compensation tax and file quarterly wage reports.
Georgia does not set a state minimum wage above the federal $7.25 for most private employers; covered employers follow FLSA minimum wage and overtime rules.
Georgia employers must pay wages when due under state payday laws; final wages timing applies on separation.
The Georgia Fair Employment Practices Act of 1978 prohibits public-employer discrimination; private employers with 15+ employees are covered by federal Title VII enforced by EEOC.
Georgia employers with three or more employees must provide workers' compensation insurance; construction subcontractors may trigger coverage at one employee.
Georgia has no statewide paid sick leave mandate for private employers; federal FMLA applies to covered employers.
Employers must register for Georgia unemployment insurance tax, file quarterly reports, and post UI workplace notices.
North Carolina follows the federal minimum wage of $7.25 for most employees; state Wage and Hour Act incorporates federal standards for overtime.
Youth employment certificates (YEC) are required for workers under 18 in non-agricultural jobs under state child-labor rules.
The North Carolina Equal Employment Practices Act declares it against public policy to discriminate in employment based on race, religion, color, national origin, age, sex, or handicap.
North Carolina employers with three or more employees must carry workers' compensation insurance; construction employers with one or more employees must cover.
North Carolina has no statewide paid sick leave mandate for private employers; employers must post federal and state workplace notices.
Employers must register with DES for unemployment insurance tax and file quarterly wage reports.
Michigan Improved Workforce Opportunity Wage Act sets state minimum wage and tipped minimum rates above the federal floor where applicable.
Nonexempt employees receive overtime at 1.5× regular rate after 40 hours in a workweek under state wage-hour rules aligned with FLSA.
The Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act prohibits employment discrimination based on religion, race, color, national origin, age, sex, height, weight, familial status, or marital status.
Michigan employers must secure workers' compensation insurance for employees or qualify as self-insured; agricultural employers with 3+ employees 35+ days/year must cover.
Michigan Paid Medical Leave Act requires eligible employees to earn one hour of sick time per 35 hours worked (or front-load); poster and recordkeeping required.
Employers must register with Michigan UIA for unemployment insurance tax and file quarterly wage reports.

items.assessment: Employers must pay the higher applicable rate; CA/NY/FL/IL/MI exceed federal floor; TX/GA/NC track federal unless local ordinance applies.

items.topic: OSHA — federal vs Cal/OSHA / MI/OSHA state plans
items.assessment: California and Michigan operate state-plan OSHA programs; construction employers in CA follow Cal/OSHA Title 8; MI MIOSHA applies to public and private sector.

items.topic: Paid leave — IL / MI vs no statewide mandate (TX, GA, NC)
items.assessment: Illinois Paid Leave for All Workers and Michigan Paid Medical Leave impose statewide accrual; tranche-2 states without mandate still require federal poster compliance.

7_confidence.title: Confidence Assessment
7_confidence.basis: 70/74 facts FOUND; 0 explicit gaps; 31 parked sources.
